items.title: Why precocious puberty is defined before age 8 in girls and 9 in boys
items.content: Population data show that pubertal onset clusters around 10.5 years in girls and 11.5 years in boys, so ages below those means by about 2.5 SD signal abnormal early activation.
keyPoints: The cutoff reflects statistical deviation from normal timing.
Girls <8 years and boys <9 years warrant evaluation.
Cutoffs help identify pathologic causes early.
items.reference: Precocious puberty is defined as secondary sexual characteristics before 8 years in girls or 9 years in boys.

items.title: How accelerated puberty differs from precocious puberty
items.content: Accelerated puberty starts at a normal age but progresses unusually fast, so it still needs evaluation even though the onset is not early.
keyPoints: Onset is within normal limits.
Progression is rapid and may be nonsequential.
Underlying lesions can drive the speed.
items.reference: Accelerated puberty means normal-age onset with rapid progression that still needs evaluation.

items.title: Why early puberty needs evaluation beyond reassurance
items.content: Early sex steroid exposure can reduce adult height and cause psychosocial distress, and evaluation helps detect structural lesions or hormone-secreting tumors.
keyPoints: Advanced bone age threatens final height.
Psychosocial effects can be significant.
Organic causes must be excluded.
items.reference: Children with early puberty should be evaluated to prevent height loss, psychosocial issues, and missed lesions.

items.title: How GDPP differs from GIPP clinically
items.content: GDPP reflects early HPG-axis activation with sequential, progressive pubertal events, whereas GIPP is sex steroid excess without HPG reactivation and often shows discordant staging.
keyPoints: GDPP is HPG-axis driven.
GIPP is independent of gonadotropins.
Discordant staging suggests GIPP.
items.reference: GDPP is sequential and HPG-axis driven; GIPP is steroid excess with nonsequential progression.

items.title: Why isosexual and heterosexual precocity are separated
items.content: Isosexual precocity matches chromosomal sex, while heterosexual precocity reflects opposite-sex characteristics and is always gonadotropin independent.
keyPoints: Isosexual: puberty consistent with gonadal sex.
Heterosexual: discordant traits.
Heterosexual precocity implies peripheral steroids.
items.reference: Isosexual precocity aligns with gonadal sex, whereas heterosexual precocity is discordant and gonadotropin independent.

items.title: How premature thelarche is distinguished from true precocity
items.content: Isolated premature thelarche is nonprogressive, lacks other secondary sexual characteristics, and shows normal growth velocity and bone age.
keyPoints: Breast changes are mild and stable.
No pubarche or uterine enlargement.
Bone age remains appropriate.
items.reference: Premature thelarche is nonprogressive with normal growth and bone age.

items.title: Why premature thelarche often remains limited to B2–B3
items.content: Breast maturation needs both estrogen and progesterone; isolated thelarche has estrogen exposure without pubertal progesterone, so maturation stalls early.
keyPoints: Estrogen drives ductal growth.
Progesterone is required for full maturation.
Lack of progesterone limits progression.
items.reference: Isolated premature thelarche has estrogen exposure without pubertal progesterone, limiting breast staging.

items.title: How adrenarche differs from pubarche
items.content: Adrenarche is a biochemical rise in adrenal androgens around age 6, whereas pubarche is the clinical appearance of hair and body odor, usually closer to age 8.
keyPoints: Adrenarche is hormonal.
Pubarche is clinical hair development.
Conversion to testosterone drives hair changes.
items.reference: Adrenarche is biochemical adrenal androgen rise; pubarche is clinical hair development.

items.title: Why bone age guides evaluation of premature pubarche
items.content: Isolated pubarche with minimal bone age advancement suggests benign premature adrenarche, while significant advancement or virilization signals pathologic androgen excess.
keyPoints: Bone age >2.5 SD prompts evaluation.
Virilization suggests pathologic causes.
Obesity-related adrenarche is common.
items.reference: Bone age and androgenization determine whether premature pubarche needs full workup.

items.title: How basal and stimulated LH identify GDPP in girls
items.content: Basal LH at or above 0.3 IU/L indicates HPG-axis activation, and a pubertal LH response to GnRH agonist confirms GDPP when basal values are low.
keyPoints: Basal LH ≥0.3 IU/L supports GDPP.
Triptorelin-stimulated LH ≥8 IU/L is pubertal.
Estradiol response can support diagnosis.
items.reference: Basal LH and stimulated LH thresholds are used to confirm GDPP in girls.

items.title: Why LH response is preferred over FSH for diagnosing GDPP
items.content: FSH response to GnRH occurs across ages, while LH response reflects true reactivation of the GnRH pulse generator.
keyPoints: FSH rises even in prepubertal children.
LH surge signifies HPG-axis activation.
LH is more tightly neuroendocrine regulated.
items.reference: LH response to GnRH, not FSH, indicates activation of the GnRH pulse generator.

items.title: How pelvic ultrasound supports puberty assessment in girls
items.content: Uterine length above 3.5 cm, pubertal uterine shape, and ovarian volume above 2.8 ml indicate estrogen exposure and onset of puberty.
keyPoints: Uterine length >3.5 cm is significant.
Ovarian volume ≥2.8 ml suggests puberty.
Endometrial echo supports estrogen effect.
items.reference: Pelvic ultrasound parameters indicate estrogen exposure and pubertal onset in girls.

items.title: Why ovarian cyst patterns help separate GDPP from GIPP
items.content: Multiple small follicles favor GDPP, while a single large follicular cyst suggests GIPP from autonomous ovarian estrogen production.
keyPoints: Multiple cysts imply HPG-driven stimulation.
Single large cyst points to GIPP.
Cyst size >9 mm favors GIPP.
items.reference: Multiple small follicles suggest GDPP, whereas a large solitary cyst suggests GIPP.

items.title: How neuroimaging is prioritized in precocious puberty
items.content: MRI is recommended for all children with GDPP, especially boys, girls with onset before 6 years, neurological symptoms, or rapid progression.
keyPoints: Boys have higher rates of CNS lesions.
Girls <6 years have higher yield.
Gelastic seizures or visual symptoms demand imaging.
items.reference: MRI is indicated in GDPP, particularly in boys, very young girls, or those with neurologic signs.

items.title: Why hypothalamic hamartomas trigger GDPP
items.content: Ectopic GnRH neurons within hamartomas escape normal inhibition and release GnRH pulses, prematurely activating the HPG axis.
keyPoints: Accessory GnRH neurons are autonomous.
GnRH pulsatility reactivates early.
HPG-axis becomes prematurely active.
items.reference: Hypothalamic hamartomas contain ectopic GnRH neurons that drive early HPG activation.

items.title: How gelastic seizures point to hypothalamic hamartoma
items.content: Sudden episodes of laughter with autonomic features and no loss of awareness are classic for hamartoma-related gelastic seizures in young children.
keyPoints: Episodes are brief and stereotyped.
Autonomic signs often accompany laughter.
Hamartoma is the leading cause.
items.reference: Gelastic seizures are most commonly caused by hypothalamic hamartoma.

items.title: Why MAS causes gonadotropin-independent precocity
items.content: Constitutive GNAS activation drives autonomous ovarian cysts and estrogen production, leading to episodic vaginal bleeding and premature thelarche.
keyPoints: Autonomous ovarian steroidogenesis occurs.
Breast changes can wax and wane.
Café-au-lait spots and fibrous dysplasia are clues.
items.reference: MAS causes autonomous ovarian cysts and estrogen excess leading to GIPP.

items.title: How familial testotoxicosis produces early virilization
items.content: Activating LH receptor mutations trigger testosterone secretion independent of LH, leading to penile growth, pubarche, and enlarged testes in boys.
keyPoints: LH receptor gain-of-function drives testosterone.
LH is suppressed despite virilization.
Testicular size increases from intratesticular testosterone.
items.reference: Familial testotoxicosis is LH receptor activation causing LH-independent testosterone production.

items.title: Why mixed precocious puberty develops in chronic GIPP
items.content: Long-standing sex steroid excess advances bone age and primes the HPG axis, so treatment of GIPP can unmask GDPP that needs GnRH agonist therapy.
keyPoints: Advanced bone age predicts HPG activation.
GIPP therapy can unmask GDPP.
Both conditions require treatment.
items.reference: Mixed precocity is GDPP superimposed on GIPP after prolonged steroid exposure.

items.title: How boys with precocious puberty are evaluated
items.content: Evaluation includes basal LH and testosterone, GnRH stimulation if needed, and targeted imaging such as MRI brain or abdominal imaging to find etiology.
keyPoints: Basal LH ≥0.3 IU/L suggests GDPP.
Testosterone elevation guides etiology.
Imaging identifies CNS or adrenal tumors.
items.reference: Boys need basal LH/testosterone, stimulation testing, and imaging to define GDPP vs GIPP.

items.title: How girls with isosexual precocity are evaluated
items.content: Basal LH and estradiol guide classification, GnRH agonist testing confirms GDPP, and pelvic ultrasound assesses uterine and ovarian maturation.
keyPoints: Basal LH and estradiol are first-line.
GnRH stimulation confirms HPG activation.
Pelvic ultrasound documents estrogen effect.
items.reference: Girls with isosexual precocity are assessed with gonadotropins, estradiol, and pelvic ultrasound.

items.title: Why GnRH agonists are first-line for GDPP
items.content: GnRH agonists reliably suppress the HPG axis, regress pubertal changes, and help preserve adult height, especially when started early.
keyPoints: They provide reversible HPG suppression.
Regression of secondary sex traits occurs.
Best height benefit when started young.
items.reference: GnRH agonists are the preferred treatment for GDPP because they effectively suppress HPG-axis activity.

items.title: How GnRH agonist therapy works and why flare can occur
items.content: Initial receptor stimulation causes a transient rise in gonadotropins and steroids, followed by receptor downregulation and sustained suppression.
keyPoints: Flare occurs in the first 1–2 weeks.
Suppression follows within weeks.
Depot formulations are used monthly or quarterly.
items.reference: GnRH agonists cause an initial flare then downregulate receptors and suppress the HPG axis.

items.title: How to monitor response to GnRH agonist therapy
items.content: Monitoring includes Tanner staging, growth velocity, basal and stimulated LH with estradiol or testosterone, and annual bone age assessment.
keyPoints: Clinical regression should be evident by 6–12 months.
Growth velocity should slow.
Hormonal suppression confirms adequate dosing.
items.reference: Monitoring includes clinical progression, growth velocity, hormone suppression, and bone age.

items.title: Why stopping GnRH agonists is usually timed around age 11
items.content: Beyond about 11 years, continued suppression may reduce final height gain because pubertal growth depends on sex steroids, so therapy is usually stopped when age-appropriate.
keyPoints: Height gain diminishes after ~11 years.
Pubertal timing should be age appropriate.
Psychosocial goals also guide timing.
items.reference: GnRH agonists are commonly discontinued around 11 years when further height benefit is minimal.

items.title: How the HPG axis recovers after stopping GnRH agonists
items.content: Gonadotropins rise within months, pubertal progression resumes in 3–6 months, and menses typically returns within 1–1.5 years in girls.
keyPoints: Recovery is reversible and expected.
Girls resume menses within about a year.
Boys progress more slowly.
items.reference: HPG-axis activity typically recovers within a year after stopping GnRH agonists.

items.title: Why GnRH agonists do not compromise peak bone mass
items.content: Long-term data show normal accrual of bone mass, but adequate calcium and vitamin D are important during therapy.
keyPoints: Bone density normalizes after therapy.
Calcium and vitamin D support bone health.
Monitoring is still prudent.
items.reference: GnRH agonist therapy does not impair peak bone mass, but calcium and vitamin D should be optimized.

items.title: How gonadotropin-independent precocity is treated
items.content: Management targets the underlying source of sex steroids, including surgery for tumors, glucocorticoids for CAH, or aromatase inhibitors/MDPA for MAS.
keyPoints: Tumors require surgical management.
CAH responds to steroid replacement.
MAS often needs antiestrogen therapy.
items.reference: GIPP treatment is etiology-specific, including tumor removal, CAH therapy, or antiestrogen strategies for MAS.

items.question: A 3-year-old girl has Tanner B3 breasts, growth acceleration, bone age 7 years, uterine enlargement, basal LH 2.3 IU/L, and GnRH-agonist LH 56 IU/L. The best diagnosis is:
items.explanation: Advanced bone age, uterine growth, and pubertal LH response indicate GDPP.
items.reference: Pubertal LH response with advanced bone age and uterine growth supports GDPP.

items.question: Which age threshold defines precocious puberty in boys?
items.explanation: Cutoffs are <8 years in girls and <9 years in boys.
items.reference: Precocious puberty is defined as pubertal signs before 9 years in boys.

items.question: A 2-year-old girl has stable Tanner B2 breasts, no pubic hair, normal growth velocity, and normal bone age. The most likely diagnosis is:
items.explanation: Nonprogressive thelarche with normal growth and bone age suggests benign premature thelarche.
items.reference: Premature thelarche is nonprogressive with normal growth and bone age.

items.question: Which feature best distinguishes GIPP from GDPP in boys?
items.explanation: Discordant penile enlargement with small testes suggests peripheral steroid excess (GIPP).
items.reference: GIPP often shows discordant staging, such as penile growth without testicular enlargement.

items.question: Heterosexual precocious puberty is best described as:
options: Secondary sexual characteristics discordant with gonadal sex
HPG-axis reactivation with pubertal LH response
items.explanation: Heterosexual precocity is opposite-sex trait development and is gonadotropin independent.
items.reference: Heterosexual precocity is discordant with gonadal sex and is gonadotropin independent.

items.question: Adrenarche refers to:
options: Biochemical rise in adrenal androgens before clinical hair development
Appearance of pubic hair due to ovarian estrogen
items.explanation: Adrenarche is the hormonal rise in DHEA/DHEAS that precedes pubarche.
items.reference: Adrenarche is a biochemical adrenal androgen rise; pubarche is clinical hair development.

items.question: A child with isolated pubic hair and bone age advanced by >2.5 SD should undergo evaluation for:
items.explanation: Marked bone age advancement or virilization suggests pathologic causes of pubarche.
items.reference: Significant bone age advancement with pubarche warrants evaluation for androgen excess.

items.question: In girls, a basal LH value most consistent with GDPP is:
items.explanation: Basal LH at or above 0.3 IU/L indicates HPG-axis activation.
items.reference: Basal LH ≥0.3 IU/L supports GDPP.

items.question: Why is LH response to GnRH preferred over FSH response for diagnosing GDPP?
options: FSH response is absent in puberty
LH is elevated in all children regardless of puberty
FSH is only secreted by the adrenal gland
items.explanation: FSH responses occur at all ages, whereas LH rises only after HPG-axis activation.
items.reference: LH response indicates activation of the GnRH pulse generator.

items.question: Which pelvic ultrasound finding best supports pubertal onset in a girl?
items.explanation: Ovarian volume ≥2.8 ml is a strong indicator of pubertal estrogen effect.
items.reference: Ovarian volume ≥2.8 ml supports pubertal onset on ultrasound.

items.question: A single ovarian cyst larger than 9 mm in a girl with early breast development suggests:
items.explanation: A solitary large cyst favors GIPP, while multiple small follicles favor GDPP.
items.reference: A single large follicular cyst points to GIPP.

items.question: Which child with GDPP has the highest priority for MRI brain?
options: Girl with onset at age 7.5 and slow progression
Girl with onset at 7.8 years and normal neuro exam
Girl with isolated thelarche and normal bone age
items.explanation: Boys have a higher prevalence of CNS lesions and should always be imaged.
items.reference: MRI is recommended for all GDPP, particularly in boys or very young girls.

items.question: A 3-year-old boy has gelastic seizures and GDPP. The most likely lesion is:
items.explanation: Gelastic seizures in young children strongly suggest hypothalamic hamartoma.
items.reference: Gelastic seizures are most commonly caused by hypothalamic hamartoma.

items.question: A girl with precocious vaginal bleeding, café-au-lait spots, and fibrous dysplasia likely has:
items.explanation: The triad of café-au-lait macules, fibrous dysplasia, and endocrinopathy fits MAS.
items.reference: MAS causes autonomous ovarian estrogen production with café-au-lait macules and fibrous dysplasia.

items.question: A boy with early virilization, enlarged testes, suppressed LH, and family history of early puberty likely has:
items.explanation: LH receptor activation causes gonadotropin-independent testosterone production with enlarged testes.
items.reference: Familial testotoxicosis is LH receptor activation causing LH-independent testosterone production.

items.question: Mixed precocious puberty most often occurs when:
options: Long-standing GIPP advances bone age and later activates HPG axis
GnRH agonist therapy is started early
The child has isolated premature thelarche
items.explanation: Chronic steroid exposure primes the HPG axis, leading to GDPP superimposed on GIPP.
items.reference: Mixed precocity is GDPP superimposed on GIPP after prolonged steroid exposure.

items.question: In boys, a basal LH ≥0.3 IU/L most strongly suggests:
items.explanation: Basal LH at or above 0.3 IU/L indicates HPG-axis activation.
items.reference: Basal LH ≥0.3 IU/L suggests GDPP in boys.

items.question: The first-line medical treatment for GDPP is:
items.explanation: GnRH agonists suppress the HPG axis and reverse pubertal progression.
items.reference: GnRH agonists are the preferred treatment for GDPP.

items.question: A known effect of initiating GnRH agonist therapy is:
options: Transient pubertal flare in the first 1–2 weeks
Rapid loss of bone density in all patients
items.explanation: Initial receptor stimulation causes a temporary rise in gonadotropins and sex steroids.
items.reference: GnRH agonists cause an initial flare before sustained suppression.

items.question: Which monitoring strategy is most appropriate for children receiving GnRH agonists?
options: Tanner staging, growth velocity, and periodic LH/estradiol or testosterone
items.explanation: Clinical and biochemical monitoring ensures adequate suppression.
items.reference: Monitoring includes pubertal staging, growth velocity, and hormone suppression.

items.question: Stopping GnRH agonist therapy is usually considered when:
options: Chronological age approaches 11 years and height benefit is minimal
Bone age equals chronological age at all times
items.explanation: Height gains are limited beyond about 11 years, so therapy is often stopped then.
items.reference: Therapy is commonly stopped around age 11 when further height benefit is minimal.

items.question: After stopping GnRH agonist therapy, menses in girls typically resumes:
items.explanation: Gonadotropin recovery is gradual, with menses returning within about a year.
items.reference: Menses typically resumes within 1–1.5 years after stopping GnRH agonists.

items.question: Which statement about GnRH agonist therapy and bone mass is most accurate?
options: Peak bone mass is generally preserved with adequate calcium and vitamin D
It prevents any bone accrual after therapy stops
It increases fracture risk in all children
items.explanation: Evidence shows no long-term compromise of peak bone mass.
items.reference: GnRH agonist therapy does not impair peak bone mass when nutrition is optimized.

items.question: Best initial treatment for precocious puberty from an adrenal tumor is:
items.explanation: Tumor-driven GIPP requires surgical removal of the source of steroids.
items.reference: GIPP treatment targets the underlying source, with surgery for tumors.

items.question: Which medication class is commonly used for precocity in McCune-Albright syndrome?
items.explanation: MAS treatment often uses antiestrogen strategies such as aromatase inhibitors or MDPA.
items.reference: MAS-related precocity can be treated with MDPA, ketoconazole, or aromatase inhibitors.

items.question: A girl with GDPP, visual impairment, and rapid pubertal progression should:
options: Undergo MRI brain to evaluate for CNS pathology
items.explanation: Neurologic signs or rapid progression increase the likelihood of CNS lesions.
items.reference: MRI is indicated in GDPP with neurologic features or rapid progression.

items.statement: Precocious puberty is defined as pubertal signs before 8 years in girls or 9 years in boys.
items.explanation: These age cutoffs are standard for clinical evaluation.
items.reference: Precocious puberty is defined as secondary sexual characteristics before 8 years in girls or 9 years in boys.

items.statement: Isolated premature thelarche is typically nonprogressive with normal bone age.
items.explanation: Stable breast development without growth acceleration is typical.
items.reference: Premature thelarche is nonprogressive with normal growth and bone age.

items.statement: GIPP is characterized by sex steroid excess independent of HPG-axis activation.
items.explanation: GIPP is peripheral and not driven by gonadotropins.
items.reference: GIPP is sex steroid excess without HPG-axis reactivation.

items.statement: Adrenarche is a biochemical rise in DHEA/DHEAS that precedes pubarche.
items.explanation: Clinical pubic hair appears later.
items.reference: Adrenarche is a biochemical adrenal androgen rise; pubarche is clinical hair development.

items.statement: Basal LH ≥0.3 IU/L in a girl suggests GDPP.
items.explanation: This threshold indicates HPG-axis activation.
items.reference: Basal LH ≥0.3 IU/L supports GDPP.

items.statement: Ovarian volume ≥2.8 ml on ultrasound supports pubertal onset in girls.
items.explanation: Ovarian volume is a sensitive marker of estrogen exposure.
items.reference: Ovarian volume ≥2.8 ml supports pubertal onset on ultrasound.

items.statement: MRI brain is recommended for all children diagnosed with GDPP.
items.explanation: CNS lesions are more common in boys and younger girls.
items.reference: MRI is indicated in GDPP, particularly in boys or very young girls.

items.statement: Gelastic seizures are strongly associated with hypothalamic hamartoma.
items.explanation: The lesion is the most common cause of gelastic seizures.
items.reference: Gelastic seizures are most commonly caused by hypothalamic hamartoma.

items.statement: GnRH agonists can cause an initial flare before suppression.
items.explanation: Early receptor stimulation temporarily raises gonadotropins.
items.reference: GnRH agonists cause an initial flare before sustained suppression.

items.statement: HPG-axis recovery after stopping GnRH agonists usually occurs within a year.
items.explanation: Gonadotropins rise within months after discontinuation.
items.reference: HPG-axis activity typically recovers within a year after stopping GnRH agonists.

items.statement: GnRH agonist therapy generally does not impair peak bone mass when nutrition is adequate.
items.explanation: Long-term data support normal bone mass accrual.
items.reference: GnRH agonist therapy does not impair peak bone mass when nutrition is optimized.

items.statement: Treatment of GIPP is tailored to the underlying source of steroid excess.
items.explanation: Tumors, CAH, and MAS require different treatments.
items.reference: GIPP treatment targets the underlying source, with surgery for tumors or medical therapy for specific causes.

items.assertion: Precocious puberty is defined as pubertal signs before 8 years in girls.
items.reason: This cutoff reflects about 2.5 standard deviations below the mean age of pubertal onset.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the cutoff.
items.reference: Precocious puberty cutoffs are based on statistical deviation from mean pubertal onset age.

items.assertion: GDPP usually shows sequential pubertal progression.
items.reason: It is driven by early activation of the HPG axis.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the pattern.
items.reference: GDPP reflects early HPG activation with sequential pubertal changes.

items.assertion: Isolated premature thelarche is typically nonprogressive.
items.reason: Progesterone exposure remains prepubertal, limiting breast maturation.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the limited progression.
items.reference: Premature thelarche remains limited because progesterone levels are prepubertal.

items.assertion: Basal LH ≥0.3 IU/L supports a diagnosis of GDPP in girls.
items.reason: LH elevation reflects reactivation of the GnRH pulse generator.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the test.
items.reference: Basal LH thresholds indicate HPG-axis activation.

items.assertion: LH response to GnRH is preferred over FSH response to diagnose GDPP.
items.reason: FSH response occurs even in prepubertal children.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains test choice.
items.reference: LH response indicates true HPG activation, while FSH responds at all ages.

items.assertion: Ovarian volume ≥2.8 ml on ultrasound suggests pubertal onset.
items.reason: Ovarian enlargement reflects estrogen exposure and follicular activity.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the imaging marker.
items.reference: Ovarian enlargement on ultrasound reflects estrogen exposure and pubertal onset.

items.assertion: MRI brain is recommended in boys with GDPP.
items.reason: Boys have a higher likelihood of CNS pathology.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the recommendation.
items.reference: Boys with GDPP have a higher prevalence of CNS lesions, so MRI is indicated.

items.assertion: Gelastic seizures suggest hypothalamic hamartoma in children with precocity.
items.reason: Hamartomas contain ectopic GnRH neurons that can drive early puberty.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the association.
items.reference: Gelastic seizures are linked to hypothalamic hamartoma and early HPG activation.

items.assertion: GnRH agonists can cause a transient flare in the first 1–2 weeks.
items.reason: Initial receptor stimulation increases gonadotropin secretion before downregulation.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the flare.
items.reference: GnRH agonists initially stimulate then suppress the HPG axis.

items.assertion: Monitoring GnRH agonist therapy includes growth velocity and hormone suppression.
items.reason: Clinical regression and biochemical suppression confirm adequate dosing.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains monitoring.
items.reference: Therapy is monitored by growth, pubertal staging, and hormone suppression.

items.assertion: GnRH agonist therapy is often discontinued around age 11.
items.reason: Further height gain is limited once pubertal growth depends on sex steroids.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains timing.
items.reference: Stopping GnRH agonists around age 11 reflects minimal further height benefit.

items.assertion: Treatment of GIPP depends on the underlying cause.
items.reason: Tumors require surgery while CAH and MAS need medical therapy.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the approach.
items.reference: GIPP management is etiology-specific, including surgery or targeted medical therapy.

items.title: Why we deconstruct puberty into onset time and progression velocity
items.content: To evaluate pubertal anomalies cleanly, separate puberty into two independent variables: the Onset Time (T0) — when the HPG axis first activates — and the Progression Velocity (Vp) — how fast the child moves through the Tanner stages. They can be abnormal independently. Precocious puberty is an anomaly of T0 (onset earlier than physiological norms — girls < 8 years, boys < 9 years), regardless of the subsequent speed. Accelerated puberty is an anomaly of Vp (onset within normal limits, but the march through Tanner stages is abnormally rapid). Holding these two axes apart is what prevents a normal starting line from disguising a dangerous speed.
keyPoints: Two independent variables: Onset Time (T0) and Progression Velocity (Vp).
Precocious = early onset (T0 abnormal), regardless of velocity.
Accelerated = normal onset but abnormally rapid progression (Vp abnormal).

items.title: Precocious puberty: an anomaly of onset (T0)
items.content: Precocious puberty is defined by an early onset of pubertal development — breast development before 8 years in girls or testicular enlargement before 9 years in boys — irrespective of how fast it then progresses. The sequence of stages is usually preserved (in girls: thelarche → pubarche → menarche). The diagnostic task is to separate central, GnRH-dependent precocious puberty (premature activation of the HPG axis) from peripheral, GnRH-independent causes (autonomous sex-steroid production). A GnRH stimulation test with baseline LH/FSH distinguishes them; if central, a brain MRI is indicated to exclude a structural lesion (e.g. a hypothalamic hamartoma), though many cases in girls are idiopathic.
keyPoints: Early onset: girls < 8 years, boys < 9 years.
Sequence usually preserved (thelarche → pubarche → menarche).
Separate central (GnRH-dependent) from peripheral; GnRH stim + LH/FSH; MRI if central.

items.title: How rapid progression velocity (Vp) flags accelerated puberty
items.content: Accelerated puberty has a normal onset (girls ≥ 8, boys ≥ 9) but an abnormally rapid progression — for example advancing a full Tanner stage in under 6 months, when the normal physiological transition from B2 to menarche takes roughly 2 to 2.5 years. Its signature is skeletal: the bone age advances dramatically faster than the chronological age (ΔBA/ΔCA much greater than 1). The progression can also become non-sequential or heavily overlapped (e.g. overlapping thelarche and rapid menarche without the usual intermediate dwell times). A steep velocity, even from a normal start, is a major red flag for a potent underlying driver.
keyPoints: Normal onset, but a full Tanner stage advances in < ~6 months (vs ~2–2.5 yr B2→menarche).
Bone age outpaces chronological age: ΔBA/ΔCA >> 1.
Progression may be non-sequential/overlapped — a red flag for a strong driver.

items.title: Case 1: the early starter (precocious puberty)
items.content: A 6-year-old girl (CA 6.2 years) presents with bilateral breast buds over 3 months and a recent growth spurt, with no CNS trauma or exogenous hormone exposure. Exam: Tanner B2, P1; growth velocity 8 cm/year (accelerated for age); bone age 8.0 years. Reasoning: the onset is unequivocally early (T0 < 8 years) — classic precocious puberty — and the sequence is preserved. The focus is differentiating central from peripheral: order a GnRH stimulation test and baseline LH/FSH; if central, obtain a brain MRI to exclude a structural anomaly, recognising that many such cases in girls are idiopathic.
keyPoints: 6.2-y girl: B2/P1, BA 8.0, GV 8 cm/yr — early onset = precocious.
Sequence preserved; workup = GnRH stim test + LH/FSH.
Brain MRI if central; many idiopathic in girls.

items.title: Case 2: the rapid progressor (accelerated puberty)
items.content: A 9-year-old girl (CA 9.0 years) had thelarche at ~8.5 years (normal onset), but over the last 6 months her breasts enlarged markedly, pubic hair appeared rapidly, and she has begun spotting. Exam: Tanner B4, P3; growth velocity 10 cm/year; bone age 11.5 years (a large advance over a short interval). Reasoning: the onset was normal, but the transition from B2 to B4 in just 6 months is abnormally fast (normal B2→menarche ~2–2.5 years). This steep velocity is the red flag: rapid progression is highly suspicious for a potent autonomous driver, so the workup must aggressively hunt for an underlying lesion — autonomous ovarian cysts or tumours (e.g. granulosa cell tumour), adrenal pathology (late-onset CAH or a virilizing tumour), or an aggressive CNS lesion driving high-amplitude, rapid GnRH pulsatility.
keyPoints: 9-y girl: normal onset (8.5 y) but B2→B4 in 6 months, BA 11.5, GV 10 cm/yr.
Steep velocity (ΔBA/ΔCA >> 1) is the red flag, not the onset.
Hunt for autonomous drivers: ovarian (granulosa cell), adrenal (CAH/virilizing tumour), aggressive CNS lesion.

items.title: How precocious and accelerated puberty differ at a glance
items.content: Onset (T0): precocious is abnormal (girls < 8, boys < 9); accelerated is normal (girls ≥ 8, boys ≥ 9). Velocity (Vp): in precocious puberty it is usually normal (can be fast); in accelerated puberty it is abnormally rapid (< ~1 year between stages). Sequence: precocious is typically sequential and ordered; accelerated can be non-sequential or heavily overlapped. Bone-age kinetics: in precocious puberty bone age advances steadily alongside chronological age; in accelerated puberty bone age advances dramatically faster than chronological age. Clinical imperative: precocious puberty → evaluate for premature HPG-axis activation (central vs peripheral); accelerated puberty → evaluate for high-output autonomous or structural lesions driving the rapid maturation.
keyPoints: Onset: precocious abnormal vs accelerated normal.
Velocity: precocious usually normal vs accelerated abnormally rapid.
Imperative: precocious → premature axis activation; accelerated → high-output lesion driving speed.

items.title: Why a normal onset does not excuse a dangerous velocity
items.content: The central lesson is that the timing of onset does not give a free pass on the velocity of progression: a normal starting line does not negate the pathology of a dangerous speed. Both precocious and accelerated puberty require rigorous endocrine evaluation, but they point the workup in different directions — precocious toward the question of why the axis activated early, and accelerated toward the question of what is driving the rapid physiological state change. Missing accelerated puberty because the onset was 'normal' is exactly the trap this framework is designed to prevent.
keyPoints: Onset timing does not excuse abnormal velocity.
Both need evaluation, but in different directions.
A 'normal onset' must not let a dangerous velocity go unworked-up.

items.question: Why does accelerated puberty, despite a normal age of onset, demand aggressive evaluation?
items.answer: Because the abnormality lies in the velocity, not the onset, and an abnormally high progression velocity implies a potent driver pushing maturation faster than physiology should allow. Normal puberty moves through the Tanner stages over years; advancing a full stage in under six months, with bone age racing ahead of chronological age, signals an autonomous, high-output source — an ovarian cyst or granulosa cell tumour, adrenal pathology such as late-onset CAH or a virilizing tumour, or an aggressive CNS lesion driving rapid GnRH pulsatility. These causes can be serious and progressive, and the steep velocity also threatens final height through accelerated epiphyseal fusion. A normal onset is therefore no reassurance: the speed itself is the pathology that must be explained.
keyPoints: The pathology is the velocity, which implies a potent autonomous driver.
Differential: ovarian/adrenal tumours, late-onset CAH, aggressive CNS lesions.
Rapid progression also threatens final height; normal onset is no reassurance.

items.question: How does the diagnostic focus differ between precocious and accelerated puberty?
items.answer: In precocious puberty the question is why the axis activated early, so the workup distinguishes central from peripheral causes: baseline LH/FSH and a GnRH stimulation test, and — if central — a brain MRI to exclude a structural lesion (with the awareness that many girls are idiopathic). In accelerated puberty the onset was normal, so the workup instead hunts for the driver of the speed: imaging and biochemistry directed at autonomous sources — pelvic ultrasound for ovarian cysts or tumours, adrenal androgens and 17-OHP for adrenal pathology and late-onset CAH, and brain imaging if a central high-amplitude driver is suspected. Bone age is read in both as a measure of how far skeletal maturation has outpaced chronological age. The unifying rule: let the abnormal variable (onset vs velocity) point the search.
keyPoints: Precocious: LH/FSH + GnRH stim test; brain MRI if central.
Accelerated: hunt the driver — pelvic USG, adrenal androgens/17-OHP, CNS imaging.
Read bone age in both; let the abnormal variable direct the workup.

items.stem: How is precocious puberty fundamentally defined?
options: By an abnormally rapid progression through Tanner stages
By an early onset of pubertal development (girls < 8, boys < 9), regardless of subsequent velocity
By a normal onset with a fast bone age
items.explanation: Precocious puberty is an anomaly of onset time (T0): development begins earlier than the physiological norm (girls < 8, boys < 9 years), irrespective of how fast it then progresses.

options: A normal age of onset but an abnormally rapid progression through Tanner stages (Vp abnormal)
items.explanation: Accelerated puberty is an anomaly of progression velocity (Vp): onset is within normal limits, but the march through Tanner stages is abnormally fast, with bone age advancing far ahead of chronological age.

items.stem: A 6.2-year-old girl has Tanner B2 breasts, bone age 8.0 years, and growth velocity 8 cm/year. What is the diagnosis and the appropriate next step?
options: Precocious puberty; obtain baseline LH/FSH and a GnRH stimulation test (and brain MRI if central)
items.explanation: Onset before 8 years is precocious puberty (an anomaly of T0). The workup differentiates central from peripheral causes with LH/FSH and a GnRH stimulation test, with brain MRI if central activation is confirmed.

items.stem: A 9-year-old girl with thelarche at 8.5 years progresses from B2 to B4 in 6 months, with bone age 11.5 years. What does this pattern most demand?
options: Reassurance, since the onset was normal
An aggressive hunt for an autonomous driver (ovarian/adrenal tumour, late-onset CAH, or CNS lesion) because the rapid velocity is the red flag
Only a bone-age film at one year
Immediate menarche suppression with no workup
items.explanation: Although onset was normal, advancing two Tanner stages in 6 months with bone age racing ahead is accelerated puberty. The steep velocity points to a potent autonomous driver that must be aggressively investigated.

items.stem: A ratio of ΔBA/ΔCA much greater than 1 indicates what?
options: Bone age lagging behind chronological age
Skeletal maturation advancing dramatically faster than chronological age — characteristic of accelerated puberty
items.explanation: ΔBA/ΔCA >> 1 means the bone age is advancing far faster than the chronological age — the skeletal signature of accelerated puberty and a threat to final height.

items.stem: Which lesion is a classic autonomous driver to seek in rapidly progressing (accelerated) puberty in a girl?
options: An ovarian granulosa cell tumour (or other autonomous ovarian/adrenal source)
A normal idiopathic process requiring no imaging
items.explanation: Rapid progression suggests a high-output autonomous source: ovarian cysts or granulosa cell tumours, adrenal pathology (late-onset CAH or virilizing tumours), or an aggressive CNS lesion — all of which the workup should target.

items.stem: Approximately how long does the normal physiological transition from Tanner B2 to menarche take?
items.explanation: Normal progression from B2 to menarche takes roughly 2 to 2.5 years; covering that span in around 6 months is abnormally rapid and defines accelerated progression.

items.stem: What is the key principle linking precocious and accelerated puberty?
options: Only the age of onset matters; velocity can be ignored
A normal onset does not excuse an abnormal velocity — both timing and speed must be evaluated
Only velocity matters; onset is irrelevant
items.explanation: The timing of onset does not give a free pass on the velocity of progression: a normal starting line does not negate the pathology of a dangerous speed, so both axes require rigorous evaluation.

items.statement: Precocious puberty is defined by an early onset of pubertal development, regardless of the subsequent progression velocity.
items.explanation: True. Precocious puberty is an anomaly of onset time (girls < 8, boys < 9), independent of how fast it then progresses.

items.statement: Accelerated puberty is characterised by an abnormally early age of onset.
items.explanation: False. Accelerated puberty has a normal age of onset; the abnormality is the rapid progression velocity through the Tanner stages.

items.statement: In accelerated puberty, bone age advances much faster than chronological age (ΔBA/ΔCA >> 1).
items.explanation: True. The hallmark of accelerated puberty is rapid skeletal maturation outpacing chronological age.

items.statement: A normal age of pubertal onset rules out significant underlying pathology.
items.explanation: False. A normal onset does not excuse an abnormally rapid velocity; accelerated puberty with normal onset can be driven by serious autonomous lesions.

items.statement: Advancing a full Tanner stage in under 6 months is a red flag for an autonomous driver of puberty.
items.explanation: True. Such rapid progression (versus the normal ~2–2.5 years from B2 to menarche) strongly suggests a potent autonomous or structural driver.

items.statement: Confirmed central precocious puberty warrants a brain MRI to exclude a structural lesion.
items.explanation: True. Once central (GnRH-dependent) precocious puberty is confirmed, brain MRI is indicated to exclude lesions such as a hypothalamic hamartoma, although many cases in girls are idiopathic.

items.assertion: A child with normal-onset but rapidly progressing puberty still requires aggressive endocrine evaluation.
items.reason: An abnormally high progression velocity suggests a potent autonomous driver, such as an ovarian, adrenal, or CNS lesion.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the assertion. Because the rapid velocity itself implies a high-output autonomous or structural driver, a normal onset provides no reassurance — which is exactly why aggressive evaluation is required.

items.title: How GDPP and GIPP differ at the node of failure
items.content: The central-versus-peripheral split is about where the pathology sits in the endocrine architecture. Gonadotropin-dependent precocious puberty (GDPP, central) is a premature reactivation of the hypothalamic GnRH pulse generator: the HPG axis is intact but switched into a mature operational state too early — so it is a loss of central inhibition. Gonadotropin-independent precocious puberty (GIPP, peripheral) is autonomous production of sex steroids (or exogenous exposure) that entirely bypasses the axis — an end-organ escape. The whole diagnostic split flows from this single question: is the driver central axis activation, or autonomous peripheral steroid?
keyPoints: GDPP (central) = premature reactivation of the GnRH pulse generator; axis intact.
GIPP (peripheral) = autonomous (or exogenous) sex steroids bypassing the axis.
One question localizes everything: central activation vs autonomous peripheral steroid.

items.title: Why GDPP shows pubertal LH while GIPP shows suppressed LH
items.content: The biochemistry follows the mechanism. In GDPP the pituitary is being driven by GnRH, so LH is pubertal — either a pubertal basal LH or a brisk pubertal LH response to GnRH (or GnRH-agonist) stimulation. In GIPP the sex steroids are made autonomously, peripheral to the axis, so they feed back to suppress the hypothalamus and pituitary: LH and FSH are low and flat and do not rise with GnRH stimulation. Therefore a pubertal stimulated LH points central, while suppressed, non-responsive gonadotropins point peripheral — this single test result is the pivot of the workup.
keyPoints: GDPP: pubertal basal LH, or a pubertal LH response to GnRH stimulation.
GIPP: suppressed, flat LH/FSH that do not respond to GnRH.
Stimulated LH is the pivotal test: pubertal = central; suppressed = peripheral.

items.title: GDPP: idiopathic and constitutional causes
items.content: Most central precocious puberty in girls is idiopathic. Idiopathic/constitutional GDPP accounts for roughly 80–90% of GDPP in females and often represents the extreme early tail of the normal physiological distribution rather than a discrete lesion. In boys the picture is reversed: idiopathic GDPP is rare (under ~10%), so a central structural lesion must be aggressively ruled out in any boy with central precocious puberty. This sex difference is why a brain MRI is essentially mandatory in central precocious puberty in boys, and more selective in girls.
keyPoints: Idiopathic GDPP = ~80–90% of central precocious puberty in girls.
Often the extreme early tail of normal, not a lesion.
In boys idiopathic GDPP is rare (<~10%) → aggressively exclude a structural lesion.

items.title: Why genetic loss of the GnRH 'brake' (or kisspeptin gain) causes GDPP
items.content: Several gene defects drive central premature activation by removing inhibition or adding drive to the GnRH pulse generator. MKRN3 (makorin RING-finger protein 3) loss-of-function is the most common genetic cause of familial GDPP: MKRN3 normally exerts a fundamental inhibitory tone on GnRH secretion, so losing this 'brake' releases early-onset puberty (note the imprinted, paternal-inheritance pattern). DLK1 (delta-like non-canonical Notch ligand 1) loss-of-function similarly removes central inhibition. KISS1 / KISS1R (kisspeptin and its receptor) gain-of-function does the opposite — pathological upregulation or autonomous firing of the kisspeptin system directly accelerates GnRH pulsatility. The theme: release a brake or press an accelerator on the pulse generator.
keyPoints: MKRN3 loss of function: most common familial GDPP — releases the GnRH 'brake' (paternally inherited).
DLK1 loss of function: also removes central inhibition.
KISS1/KISS1R gain of function: accelerates GnRH pulsatility (presses the accelerator).

items.title: GDPP: CNS structural lesions
items.content: Several CNS lesions cause central precocious puberty. The hypothalamic hamartoma is the most common structural cause — ectopic neural tissue that behaves as an autonomous, ectopic GnRH pulse generator, classically associated with gelastic (laughing) seizures. Astrocytomas and gliomas, particularly optic pathway gliomas associated with Neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF1), are important to look for. Craniopharyngiomas more often cause delayed puberty through pituitary-stalk compression but can occasionally disrupt inhibitory pathways and cause precocity. Pineal tumors are a historical association, thought to alter melatonin rhythms and remove GnRH inhibition. Any of these mandates brain imaging once central activation is confirmed.
keyPoints: Hypothalamic hamartoma: most common; ectopic GnRH generator; gelastic seizures.
Optic pathway gliomas/astrocytomas (NF1 association).
Craniopharyngioma (usually delays puberty) and pineal tumors are also implicated.

items.title: How acquired CNS insults trigger central precocious puberty
items.content: Damage to the inhibitory networks above the GnRH pulse generator can disinhibit it. Post-infectious sequelae — scarring after meningitis, encephalitis, or brain abscess — can disrupt inhibitory neural circuits and trigger central precocity. Trauma and ischemia (severe traumatic brain injury, hydrocephalus, hypoxic-ischemic events) act similarly. Iatrogenic cranial irradiation has a dose-dependent, paradoxical effect: low-dose radiation (e.g. for leukaemia) often accelerates pubertal timing by removing inhibition, whereas high-dose radiation destroys the axis and causes hypogonadism. The unifying mechanism is loss of the inhibitory tone that normally restrains the pulse generator.
keyPoints: Post-infectious scarring (meningitis/encephalitis) disrupts inhibitory circuits.
Trauma, hydrocephalus, hypoxic-ischemic injury can disinhibit the pulse generator.
Cranial irradiation: low dose accelerates puberty; high dose destroys the axis.

items.title: GIPP: gonadal causes
items.content: In peripheral precocious puberty the sex steroids come from the gonad autonomously. In girls, autonomous ovarian follicular cysts are the most common cause of GIPP — they transiently secrete high estradiol, often producing rapid breast development and a withdrawal bleed when the cyst ruptures or involutes. Ovarian tumours (granulosa cell tumours, thecomas, functioning teratomas) are rarer drivers. In boys, Leydig cell tumours secrete autonomous testosterone and typically present with asymmetric testicular enlargement, while hCG-secreting germ cell tumours drive bilateral enlargement because excess hCG cross-reacts with the LH receptor. Testicular size and symmetry therefore help localise the source.
keyPoints: Girls: autonomous ovarian follicular cyst = most common GIPP (transient estradiol, withdrawal bleed).
Ovarian tumours: granulosa cell, thecoma, functioning teratoma.
Boys: Leydig cell tumour (asymmetric testis) vs hCG germ cell tumour (bilateral via LH receptor).

items.title: GIPP: adrenal causes
items.content: The adrenal gland is a major peripheral source of androgens. Congenital adrenal hyperplasia — non-classical or late-onset classical 21-hydroxylase or 11-beta-hydroxylase deficiency — shunts steroidogenesis toward the androgen pathway, driving rapid pubarche and growth. A key localising clue in boys is that adrenal androgen excess virilises without enlarging the testes (which require gonadotropin or LH-receptor drive to grow), so virilisation with prepubertal-sized testes points adrenal/CAH or exogenous, not gonadal. Adrenal tumours (virilizing or feminizing adenomas and carcinomas) are the other adrenal cause.
keyPoints: CAH (non-classical/late-onset 21-OH or 11-beta-OH) shunts to androgens → rapid pubarche/growth.
In boys: adrenal androgens virilise WITHOUT testicular enlargement (a key localiser).
Adrenal tumours: virilizing or feminizing adenomas/carcinomas.

items.title: Why McCune-Albright and testotoxicosis cause autonomous GIPP
items.content: Two activating defects lock end-organs into autonomous hormone production. McCune-Albright syndrome is caused by an activating somatic mutation in GNAS, the gene encoding the Gs-alpha protein; this produces constitutive cAMP signalling and autonomous glandular hyperfunction, with the classic triad of GIPP, café-au-lait macules ('coast of Maine', irregular borders), and polyostotic fibrous dysplasia. Familial male-limited precocious puberty (testotoxicosis) is caused by an activating mutation in LHCGR (the LH receptor): the Leydig cells are locked 'on', producing adult levels of testosterone despite suppressed LH. Both are gain-of-function lesions that switch a receptor or its G-protein permanently on.
keyPoints: McCune-Albright: activating GNAS (Gs-alpha) → constitutive cAMP; triad GIPP + café-au-lait + fibrous dysplasia.
Testotoxicosis (FMPP): activating LHCGR → Leydig cells locked on, adult testosterone with suppressed LH.
Both are gain-of-function lesions driving autonomous hormone output.

items.title: Why hypothyroidism (and ectopic hCG) can cause precocious puberty
items.content: Some GIPP arises from hormones cross-reacting with gonadal receptors. Van Wyk-Grumbach syndrome is profound, long-standing primary hypothyroidism in which the massive TSH elevation — TSH shares a common alpha-subunit with FSH and LH — directly cross-reacts with and activates the FSH receptor, causing cystic ovaries, breast development, and, paradoxically, delayed bone age (because the hypothyroidism itself slows skeletal maturation). Ectopic hCG from tumours (classically hepatoblastoma in boys) stimulates the LH receptor to drive testosterone. Finally, exogenous exposure — iatrogenic or accidental topical testosterone gels, estrogen creams, or systemic anabolic steroids — produces peripheral precocity from outside the body entirely.
keyPoints: Van Wyk-Grumbach: severe primary hypothyroidism; high TSH cross-reacts with FSH receptor — note paradoxically DELAYED bone age.
Ectopic hCG (e.g. hepatoblastoma in boys) stimulates the LH receptor.
Exogenous: topical testosterone/estrogen creams, anabolic steroids.

items.title: How to localize the cause: the comparative map
items.content: Read the case against the comparative map. Node of failure: GDPP = early GnRH pulsatility; GIPP = autonomous sex-steroid excess. LH/FSH: GDPP pubertal (basal or stimulated); GIPP suppressed (prepubertal, non-responsive). Gonadal size in boys: GDPP causes bilateral testicular enlargement (≥ ~4 mL); GIPP shows prepubertal testes, asymmetric enlargement (Leydig tumour), or a tumour mass — and bilateral enlargement with suppressed LH suggests an hCG-driven or LH-receptor-activating cause. Key genetics: GDPP — MKRN3, DLK1, KISS1; GIPP — GNAS (McCune-Albright), LHCGR (testotoxicosis). Common structural drivers: GDPP — hypothalamic hamartoma, glioma; GIPP — ovarian cysts, Leydig cell tumours, adrenal masses.
keyPoints: LH/FSH: pubertal = central; suppressed/flat = peripheral.
Boys' testes: bilateral enlargement = central (or hCG/LHCGR); asymmetric = Leydig tumour; prepubertal + virilized = adrenal/exogenous.
Genetics: MKRN3/DLK1/KISS1 (central) vs GNAS/LHCGR (peripheral).

items.question: Why does a GnRH stimulation test show a pubertal LH rise in GDPP but a flat, suppressed response in GIPP?
items.answer: Because the two conditions act on opposite sides of the pituitary. In GDPP the disorder is upstream: the hypothalamic GnRH pulse generator has been prematurely reactivated and is driving the pituitary, so giving exogenous GnRH (or a GnRH agonist) elicits a brisk, pubertal LH rise — the axis is primed and responsive. In GIPP the sex steroids are produced autonomously in the periphery (gonad, adrenal, or exogenous), and that steroid excess feeds back negatively on the hypothalamus and pituitary, switching off endogenous GnRH and gonadotropins; the pituitary is therefore suppressed and does not respond to a GnRH challenge. So a pubertal stimulated LH localises the problem to a driven central axis, while a flat, suppressed LH localises it to an autonomous peripheral source — making this test the decisive fork in the workup.
keyPoints: GDPP: the pulse generator drives a primed pituitary → brisk LH rise to GnRH.
GIPP: autonomous peripheral steroids suppress the axis → flat, non-responsive LH.
The stimulated-LH result localises central vs peripheral.

items.question: How do testicular examination findings help localize the cause of precocious puberty in a boy?
items.answer: Testicular size and symmetry map onto the source of androgen. Bilateral testicular enlargement (≥ ~4 mL) indicates gonadotropin (or gonadotropin-like) drive to both testes — central GDPP, or an hCG-secreting tumour or activating LH-receptor mutation (testotoxicosis) stimulating the LH receptor bilaterally. Asymmetric or unilateral enlargement, often with a palpable mass, suggests a Leydig cell tumour producing testosterone locally on one side. Prepubertal-sized testes despite clear virilisation indicate an androgen source that does not act on the testis — adrenal androgen excess (such as CAH or an adrenal tumour) or exogenous testosterone, because those virilise without providing the gonadotropin/LH-receptor signal needed to grow the testes. Combining this with the LH/FSH result and the steroid profile usually pinpoints the cause.
keyPoints: Bilateral enlargement → central, or hCG/LH-receptor-driven.
Asymmetric/unilateral mass → Leydig cell tumour.
Prepubertal testes + virilization → adrenal (CAH/tumour) or exogenous androgen.

items.stem: What fundamentally distinguishes GDPP from GIPP?
options: GDPP is always tumoral; GIPP is always idiopathic
GDPP is premature central GnRH-driven activation (pubertal LH); GIPP is autonomous peripheral sex-steroid excess (suppressed LH)
GDPP only occurs in boys; GIPP only in girls
items.explanation: GDPP is gonadotropin-dependent — premature reactivation of the GnRH pulse generator with pubertal LH — whereas GIPP is gonadotropin-independent autonomous sex-steroid production that suppresses LH/FSH.

items.stem: What is the most common genetic cause of familial gonadotropin-dependent precocious puberty?
items.explanation: MKRN3 loss of function is the most common genetic cause of familial GDPP: MKRN3 normally inhibits GnRH secretion, so losing this brake releases early central activation (with a paternally-inherited, imprinted pattern).

items.stem: Which is the most common CNS structural lesion causing central precocious puberty, classically associated with gelastic seizures?
items.explanation: A hypothalamic hamartoma is the most common structural cause of GDPP; it behaves as an ectopic, autonomous GnRH pulse generator and is classically associated with gelastic (laughing) seizures.

items.stem: What is the most common cause of gonadotropin-independent precocious puberty in girls?
items.explanation: Autonomous ovarian follicular cysts are the most common cause of GIPP in girls, transiently secreting high estradiol and often causing rapid breast development with a withdrawal bleed on cyst involution.

items.stem: McCune-Albright syndrome is caused by which molecular defect, and what is its classic triad?
options: LHCGR loss of function; triad of short stature, deafness, cataracts
Activating somatic GNAS (Gs-alpha) mutation; triad of GIPP, café-au-lait macules, and polyostotic fibrous dysplasia
MKRN3 loss of function; triad of seizures, obesity, diabetes
21-hydroxylase deficiency; triad of salt-wasting, virilization, hypotension
items.explanation: McCune-Albright results from an activating somatic GNAS mutation causing constitutive cAMP signalling and autonomous glandular hyperfunction, with the triad of GIPP, café-au-lait macules, and polyostotic fibrous dysplasia.

items.stem: Familial male-limited precocious puberty (testotoxicosis) results from which defect?
options: An activating mutation of the LH receptor (LHCGR), locking Leydig cells 'on' with suppressed LH
items.explanation: Testotoxicosis is caused by an activating LHCGR mutation: the Leydig cells autonomously produce adult-level testosterone despite suppressed LH — a peripheral, gonadotropin-independent cause.

items.stem: In Van Wyk-Grumbach syndrome, what drives the precocious puberty, and what is the characteristic bone-age finding?
options: Markedly elevated TSH cross-reacting with the FSH receptor (severe primary hypothyroidism); paradoxically DELAYED bone age
An ovarian tumour; normal bone age
items.explanation: Profound primary hypothyroidism raises TSH so much that it cross-reacts with the FSH receptor (shared alpha-subunit), causing cystic ovaries and breast development — yet the hypothyroidism itself slows skeletal maturation, so bone age is paradoxically delayed.

items.stem: A boy with precocious puberty has asymmetric (unilateral) testicular enlargement. What is the most likely cause?
options: A Leydig cell tumour producing autonomous testosterone
items.explanation: Asymmetric/unilateral testicular enlargement, often with a mass, points to a Leydig cell tumour secreting testosterone locally. Central causes and hCG-driven tumours typically enlarge both testes.

items.stem: How does cranial irradiation affect pubertal timing?
options: It always delays puberty regardless of dose
Low-dose radiation often accelerates pubertal timing, whereas high-dose radiation destroys the axis
It has no effect on the HPG axis
It only affects the gonads, not the brain
items.explanation: Cranial irradiation has a dose-dependent, paradoxical effect: low doses often disinhibit and accelerate puberty (central precocity), while high doses damage the axis and cause hypogonadism.

items.stem: A boy is clearly virilized but his testes remain prepubertal in size. Where is the androgen source most likely to be?
options: The adrenal gland (e.g. CAH or adrenal tumour) or an exogenous source
items.explanation: Virilization with prepubertal-sized testes indicates an androgen source that does not stimulate testicular growth — adrenal androgen excess (CAH or adrenal tumour) or exogenous testosterone — because testicular enlargement requires gonadotropin or LH-receptor drive.

items.statement: GDPP is characterised by a pubertal LH response to GnRH stimulation.
items.explanation: True. In central (gonadotropin-dependent) precocious puberty the primed axis produces a pubertal LH rise to GnRH stimulation.

items.statement: In GIPP, LH and FSH are suppressed and do not respond to GnRH stimulation.
items.explanation: True. Autonomous peripheral sex steroids feed back to suppress the axis, so gonadotropins are low and flat and do not rise with GnRH.

items.statement: MKRN3 normally inhibits GnRH secretion, and loss-of-function mutations cause early central puberty.
items.explanation: True. MKRN3 exerts an inhibitory 'brake' on GnRH; loss of function releases that brake and is the commonest genetic cause of familial GDPP.

items.statement: A hypothalamic hamartoma acts as an ectopic, autonomous GnRH pulse generator.
items.explanation: True. The hamartoma is ectopic neural tissue that secretes GnRH autonomously, causing central precocious puberty, often with gelastic seizures.

items.statement: McCune-Albright syndrome is caused by a loss-of-function mutation in GNAS.
items.explanation: False. It is an activating (gain-of-function) somatic GNAS mutation causing constitutive cAMP signalling and autonomous glandular hyperfunction.

items.statement: In Van Wyk-Grumbach syndrome the bone age is paradoxically delayed despite the precocious puberty.
items.explanation: True. Severe primary hypothyroidism drives the precocity via TSH cross-reacting with the FSH receptor, but the hypothyroidism itself slows skeletal maturation, so bone age is delayed.

items.statement: Bilateral symmetric testicular enlargement suggests a central (or hCG/LH-receptor-driven) cause rather than a Leydig cell tumour.
items.explanation: True. Bilateral enlargement implies drive to both testes (central GnRH, hCG, or an activating LH receptor), whereas a Leydig cell tumour typically enlarges one testis asymmetrically.

items.assertion: A virilized boy with prepubertal-sized testes should be evaluated for an adrenal or exogenous androgen source.
items.reason: Adrenal androgen excess (e.g. CAH) and exogenous testosterone virilize without enlarging the testes, which require gonadotropin or LH-receptor stimulation to grow.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the assertion. Because testicular growth needs gonadotropin/LH-receptor drive, virilization with prepubertal testes indicates an androgen source acting outside the testis — adrenal or exogenous — which is exactly why those are the targets of evaluation.
